topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the printed memo, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh fluorescent lights of the breakroom. Her hand, clutching a lukewarm coffee mug, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. The words detailing her reassignment to the Acquisitions team seemed to swim before her eyes. She reread the section about the new reporting structure three times, her breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. A strange, hollow feeling settled in her chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. She slowly lowered the paper, the sound of the rustling paper amplifying the sudden silence in the room. "Acquisitions," she murmured to herself, the word tasting foreign and unsettling on her tongue.

[story 2]

Mark felt the floor tilt beneath him as he processed the official notification. He stood by the window, the cityscape outside suddenly feeling distant and irrelevant. His knuckles were white where he gripped the edge of the windowsill. He kept blinking, trying to force the information into a coherent shape, but it remained fragmented, a series of disconnected images flashing behind his eyelids. When his supervisor finally approached, his voice was too cheerful, too steady. Mark managed a tight, almost strained smile, nodding mechanically, though his internal landscape felt utterly derailed. He just wanted to look away, to retreat into the anonymity of the glass.

[story 3]

When Sarah finally read the email detailing the department move, she froze mid-sip of her water. The liquid sloshed precariously in the glass, and she had to consciously steady her hand before it tipped over. Her gaze fixed on the screen, unable to absorb the details. A deep, cold sensation washed over her, starting in her stomach and radiating outward. She pushed her chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal on the floor echoing loudly in the quiet office. "Wait," she managed, her voice sounding unnaturally high and thin. She couldn't seem to focus on the next sentence; everything else in the room had dissolved into a muted, indistinct background noise.
